metadata.description: Hand-labeled answer key for evaluating the RAG triage system. For each query failure it records the category it SHOULD be classified as, and the IDs of past failures a human would consider genuinely similar (same root-cause pattern).
metadata.how_it_is_used: eval_retrieval.py checks how many expected_similar_ids appear in the retrieved top-k (recall@k). eval_classification.py checks predicted category vs expected_category.
metadata.note: DRAFT built from the corpus similarity pairs. YOU are the judge — review each entry and correct any 'similar' relationship you disagree with. The 'why' field is a human note, not used by code.

labels.why: Both are async race conditions: the test asserts before an async event (webhook / email) is processed.
Both are UI wait-timeouts: element renders but just after the wait window under slow CI.
Both are order-dependent tests: shared state (cache / DB rows) leaks from a prior test with no teardown.
Both are nondeterministic-timing/seed flakes: unseeded RNG and wall-clock timing near a boundary.
Both are intermittent external-dependency failures (third-party 503 / timeout) that pass on rerun.

Both are Postgres connection-limit problems in the environment (pool exhausted / max_connections). NOTE: F041 also shows a Postgres connection error but its root cause is a wrong PORT in config — that is the retrieval TRAP we want to catch, so it is deliberately NOT listed here.
Both are OOMKilled pods: memory limit too small for the workload (exit 137).
Both are transient network/readiness failures in the cluster (DNS restart / pod not Ready).
Both are disk-full (ENOSPC) failures from uncleaned artifacts/images on the CI node.
Both are API schema-change regressions: a field was renamed/nested and the consumer parser broke.
Both are null/undefined regressions after a refactor: caller assumed a value that is now missing.
Both are off-by-one / boundary regressions from a changed comparison or slice bound.
Both are dependency-upgrade regressions: a bumped library removed/changed an API the code used.
Both are missing-required-field data problems: input omitted a field the parser/validator required.
Both are encoding problems: input has characters/encoding the reader/writer cannot handle.
Both are DB-constraint violations from bad input data (duplicate key / missing foreign-key reference).
Both are empty/boundary data conditions: empty result set / zero denominator with no guard.
Both are wrong-endpoint config: bad port / malformed URL. NOTE: F041's error text resembles F011/F012 (env), which is the trap for retrieval.
Both are feature-flag misconfigurations: flag defaulted off, so the wrong code path ran.
Both are version-pin misconfigurations: config pins a schema/model version that no longer matches reality.
